sanskrit_devanagari:
अकीर्तिं चापि भूतानि कथयिष्यन्ति तेऽव्ययाम् ।
सम्भावितस्य चाकीर्तिर्मरणादतिरिच्यते ॥ ३४ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
akīrtiṁ cāpi bhūtāni kathayiṣyanti te 'vyayām |
sambhāvitasya cākīrtir maraṇād atiricyate || 34 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (IAST text body identical to vedabase)

exact (academic critical edition; Bhg_02.034 = MBh_06,024.034). Note: GRETIL CE drops the leading 'ca' of pāda c — it reads 'sambhāvitasya cākīrtir maraṇād atiricyate' (the 'ca' moves into 'cākīrtir'). Modern editions identical.

anvaya.meaning: of one held in honour / of the respected one

translations.verbatim_quote: People will always speak of your infamy, and for a respectable person, dishonor is worse than death.
People will speak of you as a coward and a deserter. For a respectable person, infamy is worse than death.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Both as friend and philosopher to Arjuna, Lord Kṛṣṇa now gives His final judgment regarding Arjuna's refusal to fight. The Lord says, 'Arjuna, if you leave the battlefield before the battle even begins, people will call you a coward.'

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 2.34 Not only will there be the giving up of your duty and fame, but bhutani, people; ca api, also; kathayisyanti, will speak; te, of your; avyayam, unending, perpetual; akrtim, infamy. Ca, and; sambhavitasya, to an honoured person, to a person honoured with such epithets as 'virtuous', 'heroic', etc.; akirtih, infamy; atiricyate, is worse than; maranat, death.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads 'avyayām' (imperishable) as the load-bearing word: the infamy is not transient gossip but a perpetual signal that attaches to the actor's name. The verse is structured as an additive sequence — beyond the loss of duty (2.33) come the active speaking-against by 'all people' (bhūtāni). Shankara is careful that 'sambhāvitasya' (of one held in honour) restricts the claim: dishonour-worse-than-death holds for the actor whose previous standing was high. The verse does not say infamy is universally worse than death; it says it is worse for the actor whose name was previously credited with virtues.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 2.34 You will then incur not merely the loss of all happiness and honour but will be the object of disrespect by all people, the qualifies and even the unqualified, for all time. They will ridicule you saying, 'When the battle began, Arjuna ran away.'
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja reads 'bhūtāni' broadly: not just peers, not just kṣatriyas — all beings, qualified and unqualified, for all time. The infamy is universal-in-audience and unending-in-time. Ramanuja anticipates Arjuna's defence ('but I withdrew out of compassion, not fear') and pre-empts it: that nuance will not survive into the public account. The actual signal — 'when the battle began, Arjuna ran' — is what propagates. Ramanuja is doing rhetorical work here: the verse is not vanity; it is signal-theory. What you do under pressure is the data the world has, and the world will not adjudicate your motives. The 'sambhāvitasya' clause restricts to those whose prior honour creates the asymmetry: there is no analogous infamy-worse-than-death claim for an unknown actor.

positions.rendering: speak of you as a coward and a deserter
unending, perpetual infamy — avyayām qualifies the infamy as imperishable, the same word used for the Self in 2.21
the object of disrespect by all people, the qualified and even the unqualified, for all time

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: The Sanskrit 'avyayām' is striking because it is the same word used for the imperishable Self in 2.17 and 2.21 ('avyayam idam'). Krishna deploys it here in mirror: just as the Self is avyaya (imperishable), the infamy from quitting a duty mid-execution is avyaya. The reflection is doctrinally sharp — what the actor cannot harm in others (the imperishable Self), they can manufacture as their own imperishable signal-pattern in the world. Modern translations flatten avyayām to 'always' or drop it. Shankara and Ramanuja preserve it as 'unending.' The translation choice matters: 'always speak of your infamy' is moralistic; 'imperishable infamy attaches to your name' is structural. The verse is making the second claim, and the rhyme with the imperishable Self is intentional.

literal_meaning: And people will recount your imperishable disrepute; for one held in honour, dishonour is worse than death.
traditional_meaning_consensus: The verse continues the if-then chain from 2.33. The kīrti-loss in 2.33 develops into avyayām akīrtim (imperishable disrepute) here. Two doctrinal moves: (a) avyayām — imperishable — mirrors the imperishable-Self language of 2.17/2.21 — what cannot damage the Self can manufacture, in the actor, an imperishable signal-pattern; (b) the dishonour-worse-than-death claim is restricted to 'sambhāvitasya' — those previously held in honour; the asymmetry exists because prior honour creates the dishonour-gap. Shankara and Ramanuja both restrict the claim carefully. The verse is not generic concern-about-reputation; it is a structural claim about what action under duty signals. The kṣatriya-frame is preserved (sambhāvita-warriors, peer-witness) but the underlying claim — what you do under pressure becomes the long-lived signal others use to model you — generalises to any role where reputation is built on observable conduct under load.
